title: Corrupted image when pulling by multiple instances
body:
### Issue Description

When there are multiple instances of podman pulling the same image at the same time, the downloaded image can become corrupt.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman rmi <image>`
2. ``` for i in `seq 10`; do podman pull <image> & disown ; done```
2. `podman run --rm <image>`


### Describe the results you received

The image gets corrupted from time to time, this is an error from podman run
```
ERRO[0000] Image<> exists in local storage but may be corrupted (remove the image to resolve the issue): size for layer "43ae075764670fec7e6890628269ca14106e94bd185dad0bd1963e7bd4eb1c60" is unknown, failing getSize()
```

### Describe the results you expected

The container should run without any errors or additional pulls.

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

I am pulling the image from JFrog Artifactory but I don't expect this to be the issue.

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body: Please test with the latest podman version v4.6, we do not support older versions upstream.

How am I supposed to install latest podman on RHEL8.6?
Should I rather open this with RedHat support?

> Should I rather open this with RedHat support?

I think that's a good approach given you reported against RHEL.  Thanks a lot!

We can leave the issue here open as I think we should still investigate when we find time.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
useless activity for a useless bot
Bot basically causes us to relook at an issue, without it, the issue will disappear behind newer issues

Just wanted to say this problem still exists in podman version 5.7.1. 
It's simple to replicate as described. Start multiple instances pulling the same image.

This is a common pattern to have multiple instances for the same image. The problem is that the layers become corrupted and are not seen as prunable. To safely reclaim the space you need to nuke the storage and re-pull in proper order.
